key_pathways: Quorum sensing regulation of toxin synthesis

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron stimulates C. difficile growth and colony formation in a dose-dependent manner.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Exposure to Fe3+ increased CFU/mL up to13.8-fold at100 µM in strain9689; co-incubation with iron chelator DFO significantly reduced CFU (Figure1a).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron upregulates toxin gene expression (tcdA and tcdB) and increases toxin A protein production.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: qRT-PCR showed tcdA and tcdB mRNA levels increased2.2-fold and3.3-fold, respectively, with50 µM Fe3+; Western blot showed toxin A increased up to8.6-fold in a dose-dependent manner (Figure1b,c).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Increase in toxin A is partially attributable to increased bacterial cell density rather than per-cell upregulation.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: When toxin A levels were normalized to CFU, no significant change was observed after Fe3+ treatment (Figure1d).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron reduces susceptibility of C. difficile to metronidazole by elevating minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs).
biological_mechanisms.evidence: In strain9689,50 µM Fe3+ increased metronidazole MIC from0.17 µg/mL to1.38 µg/mL (8-fold) at48 h; co-treatment with DFO restored MIC to control levels (Figure2a, Figure3d).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron also increases MICs of fidaxomicin but does not affect vancomycin MICs.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Fidaxomicin MIC increased6.7-fold at24 h with50 µM Fe3+ in strain9689; vancomycin MICs remained unchanged even at48 h (Figure2b,c, Supplementary Tables S3 and S4).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: The iron chelator deferoxamine effectively reverses iron-induced growth, toxin production, and metronidazole resistance.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: DFO alone reduced CFU to45.8% of control at50 µM; DFO co-treatment decreased toxin A from5.2-fold to2.1-fold of control; DFO resensitized C. difficile to metronidazole, reducing MIC back to baseline (Figure3b-d).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron chelation by DFO was confirmed by CAS colorimetric assay, while metronidazole exhibited only weak iron-chelating ability at high concentration and vancomycin had none.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: DFO and ferrichrome (positive control) significantly reduced A650 in CAS assay; vancomycin had no effect; metronidazole showed chelation only at1000 µg/mL (Figure3a).

key_findings: Excess iron (Fe3+) stimulates C. difficile growth in a dose-dependent manner, with CFU increase up to13.8-fold at100 µM.
Iron exposure increases expression of toxin genes tcdA and tcdB and production of toxin A protein.
The increase in toxin production is mainly due to increased bacterial cell number, not elevated per-cell toxin expression.
Iron reduces susceptibility to metronidazole (up to8-fold MIC increase) and fidaxomicin, but vancomycin susceptibility remains unchanged.
The iron chelator deferoxamine effectively reverses iron-induced growth stimulation, toxin production, and metronidazole resistance.
In the hypervirulent strain R20291 (ribotype027), metronidazole resistance is intrinsic and not further increased by iron.
Iron may play a role in clinical treatment failure of metronidazole in CDI patients with iron overload.
Vancomycin remains a reliable alternative for CDI treatment in iron-overloaded patients.
relevance_to_research_topic: This paper directly investigates the effects of iron on Clostridioides difficile growth, toxin production, and antibiotic susceptibility, providing in vitro mechanistic evidence that excess iron promotes C. difficile virulence and reduces metronidazole efficacy. These findings support the hypothesis that high-iron diet could exacerbate CDI by enhancing pathogen growth and toxin production, and by impairing antibiotic treatment response. The paper also highlights the potential of iron chelation therapy as a strategy to counteract these effects, which is directly relevant to the proposed research on diet–gut microbiota–host interactions in CDI.
